GlobalG.A.P. Certification and GS1 Digital Link Integration in South African DPPs

This article explores the critical role of GlobalG.A.P. certification and GS1 Digital Link integration in enhancing the transparency and traceability of agricultural products within South Africa's Digital Product Passport (DPP) framework. It delves into the foundational aspects of GlobalG.A.P., the technical mechanisms of GS1 Digital Link, and their combined impact on ensuring compliance, market access, and consumer trust in the South African agricultural sector.

1. GlobalG.A.P. Overview: Founding, Governance, and Global Adoption

GlobalG.A.P., originally established in 1997 as EurepGAP by a consortium of European retailers, emerged as a direct response to escalating consumer concerns regarding food safety, environmental impact, and worker welfare within the agricultural supply chain [1]. The initiative aimed to harmonize diverse national standards into a unified framework for Good Agricultural Practice (G.A.P.). In 2007, EurepGAP was rebranded as GlobalG.A.P. to reflect its expanding international reach and influence beyond Europe [2].

The organization operates under the governance of FoodPLUS GmbH, a non-profit entity dedicated to fostering the global adoption of safe, socially, and environmentally responsible farming practices. Its mission is underpinned by a collaborative approach, engaging stakeholders across the supply chain to develop cost-effective and value-adding assurance and benchmarking solutions [2].

GlobalG.A.P. standards are now applied in over 130 countries worldwide, making it one of the most widely recognized farm assurance schemes globally. The Integrated Farm Assurance (IFA) standard for fruit and vegetables alone covers more than 4.2 million hectares of certified production annually. As of December 2022, the GlobalG.A.P. system encompassed 196,245 certified producers across 139 countries, underscoring its significant global footprint in promoting responsible agriculture [3].

2. GlobalG.A.P. IFA (Integrated Farm Assurance) Standard Version 6

The Integrated Farm Assurance (IFA) standard is GlobalG.A.P.'s flagship certification program, providing a comprehensive framework for responsible farming. Version 6 (IFA v6) represents a significant evolution, designed to be more flexible, outcome-oriented, and digitally integrated. IFA v6 for fruit and vegetables addresses a broad spectrum of critical aspects in primary production, from pre-harvest activities to post-harvest handling. Key topics covered include traceability, food safety, workers' health, safety, and welfare, biodiversity and habitats, energy efficiency, waste management, fertilizers and biostimulants, water management, integrated pest management, and plant protection products [4].

IFA v6 is available in two parallel editions: IFA v6 Smart and IFA v6 GFS. IFA v6 Smart is tailored for the majority of producers, embodying the core GlobalG.A.P. philosophy of continuous improvement and flexibility. IFA v6 GFS, conversely, is designed for producers requiring certification with recognition from the Global Food Safety Initiative (GFSI), ensuring alignment with broader food safety benchmarks [4]. This dual-edition approach accommodates diverse market demands while maintaining robust assurance standards.

A notable innovation in IFA v6 is the introduction of a continuous improvement plan at the farm level. This systematic approach encourages producers to analyze current practices, set measurable goals for optimization, implement targeted measures, and review progress annually through a Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle. This fosters ongoing enhancements in efficiency and sustainability, contributing to the long-term viability of farming operations [4]. Compliance with IFA v6 is verified through annual audits conducted by independent and accredited third-party certification bodies.

3. GGN (GlobalG.A.P. Number) as a Unique Producer Identifier

Central to the GlobalG.A.P. certification system is the GGN (GlobalG.A.P. Number), a unique 13-digit identification number assigned to every producer or producer group registered within the GlobalG.A.P. certification system. The GGN serves as a critical identifier, enabling transparent verification of certification status through the GlobalG.A.P. IT platform [5]. This unique number is instrumental in upholding the rigorous transparency requirements that underpin the integrity of the GlobalG.A.P. scheme throughout the agricultural supply chain.

The GGN provides a standardized and globally recognized means of identifying certified producers, facilitating traceability and accountability. It allows buyers, retailers, and consumers to quickly and reliably ascertain whether a producer adheres to GlobalG.A.P. standards. This transparency is vital in an era where consumers increasingly demand verifiable assurances about the origin, safety, and sustainability of their food products.

Producers can utilize their GGN on product packaging, sales documents, and other promotional materials to communicate their certified status. The GlobalG.A.P. IT platform, accessible via the GGN, provides a public interface for checking the validity of certificates, thereby reinforcing trust in the certification process [5].

4. GS1 Digital Link Standard: Encoding GTIN, GGN, and Batch/Lot

The GS1 Digital Link standard represents a paradigm shift in product identification, transforming traditional static barcodes into dynamic, web-enabled identifiers. It achieves this by encoding GS1 identifiers, such as the Global Trade Item Number (GTIN), along with other critical data like the GGN and batch/lot numbers, into a web address (URI) [6]. This "web-enabling" of product data allows for a single scan to provide access to a rich array of product-specific information, far beyond what a traditional barcode can convey.

A typical GS1 Digital Link URI for an agricultural product might incorporate the GTIN to identify the product type, the GGN to identify the certified producer, and specific batch or lot numbers for granular traceability. For example, a QR code on a South African citrus product could encode a URI that, when scanned, directs to a Digital Product Passport (DPP) containing not only the product's GTIN but also the GGN of the farm where it was grown and the specific batch/lot information for that harvest [6]. This level of detail is crucial for robust traceability systems and for complying with stringent regulatory requirements.

The standard provides a flexible and extensible framework, allowing for the inclusion of various data attributes relevant to the product and its journey through the supply chain. This includes, but is not limited to, expiry dates, serial numbers, and links to sustainability credentials or certification details. By leveraging HTTP URIs, GS1 Digital Link facilitates seamless integration with digital platforms, databases, and DPP systems, making product information universally accessible and actionable [6].

5. How a GlobalG.A.P. Certificate is Hashed and Linked to a DPP

The integration of GlobalG.A.P. certification into a Digital Product Passport (DPP) framework relies on robust mechanisms for verifying the authenticity and integrity of certification data. While the GlobalG.A.P. IT platform provides a public API for accessing certification status, the concept of "hashing" a certificate adds an immutable layer of trust and security [5].

When a GlobalG.A.P. certificate is issued, its unique data (e.g., certificate ID, producer GGN, scope, validity dates) can be processed through a cryptographic hash function, such as SHA-256. This process generates a fixed-size alphanumeric string, or "hash," that is unique to that specific certificate data. Any alteration, no matter how minor, to the original certificate data would result in a completely different hash value, thereby immediately indicating tampering [7].

This hash can then be embedded within the DPP, serving as a digital fingerprint of the certificate. When a buyer or regulator scans a product's DPP, the system can retrieve the embedded hash. This hash can then be compared against a hash generated from the official GlobalG.A.P. certificate data, potentially retrieved via the GlobalG.A.P. Public Bookmarking API [5]. A match confirms the certificate's authenticity and integrity. This mechanism provides a high degree of assurance, preventing fraudulent claims and enhancing the overall trustworthiness of the DPP system.

6. The GLOBALG.A.P. CERT System and API Integration with DPP Registries

The GLOBALG.A.P. CERT system is the overarching digital infrastructure that manages and maintains all GlobalG.A.P. certification data. It serves as the authoritative source for information on certified producers, their GGNs, and the validity of their certificates. To facilitate transparency and interoperability, GlobalG.A.P. provides a Public Bookmarking API [5]. This API allows authorized third-party systems, including DPP registries, to programmatically access and display public certification data.

Integration with DPP registries is crucial for creating a seamless flow of verifiable information. When a DPP system integrates with the GLOBALG.A.P. API, it can query the certification status of a producer using their GGN. This real-time verification capability ensures that the information presented in a DPP is always current and accurate. For instance, if a producer's certificate expires or is revoked, the DPP system can reflect this change almost instantaneously, preventing the misrepresentation of compliance status.

The API integration enables automated data exchange, reducing manual data entry errors and streamlining the verification process for all stakeholders in the supply chain. This is particularly important for large-scale operations and for meeting the demands of international trade, where rapid and reliable verification of certifications is essential for market access and regulatory compliance.

7. South African Adoption: Citrus SA, Hortgro, FPEF Membership and GlobalG.A.P. Coverage

South Africa, a significant player in global agricultural exports, has seen substantial adoption of GlobalG.A.P. standards, particularly within its export-oriented sectors. The country's National Technical Working Group (NTWG) for GlobalG.A.P. for fruit and vegetables is hosted by the Citrus Growers Association (CGA), highlighting the citrus industry's proactive engagement with international standards [8].

Key industry bodies such as the Citrus Growers Association (CGA), Hortgro (representing the pome and stone fruit industries), and the Fresh Produce Exporters' Forum (FPEF) are deeply involved in promoting and facilitating GlobalG.A.P. adoption among their members [9]. These organizations recognize that adherence to GlobalG.A.P. is often a prerequisite for accessing lucrative international markets, especially in the European Union and the United Kingdom, which have stringent import requirements.

The Sustainability Initiative of South Africa (SIZA) also plays a complementary role, aligning local sustainability efforts with global best practices, including those championed by GlobalG.A.P. [10]. The presence of accredited certification bodies like SGS South Africa (Pty) Ltd. further supports the implementation and auditing of GlobalG.A.P. standards within the country [11]. This robust ecosystem of industry support, regulatory alignment, and certification infrastructure underscores South Africa's commitment to producing safe, sustainable, and globally compliant agricultural products.

8. Comparison: GlobalG.A.P. vs LEAF Marque vs SPRING vs Rainforest Alliance

While GlobalG.A.P. is a comprehensive farm assurance standard, other certifications address specific aspects of sustainable agriculture. Understanding their differences and overlaps is crucial for producers and buyers alike.

GlobalG.A.P.
A broad, internationally recognized farm assurance standard covering food safety, environmental protection, worker health and safety, and animal welfare across various agricultural sectors. It focuses on Good Agricultural Practices (G.A.P.) and provides a framework for continuous improvement [2].
LEAF Marque
Developed by Linking Environment And Farming (LEAF), this standard emphasizes Integrated Farm Management (IFM), a whole-farm approach to sustainable agriculture. It focuses heavily on environmental protection, biodiversity enhancement, and efficient resource use [12]. While GlobalG.A.P. includes environmental criteria, LEAF Marque has a more dedicated focus on ecological aspects.
SPRING (Sustainable Program for Irrigation and Groundwater Use)
This is an add-on module to the GlobalG.A.P. IFA standard, specifically designed to address responsible water management. It provides criteria for sustainable irrigation practices, protection of water sources, and efficient water use on farms [4]. SPRING is not a standalone certification but rather enhances the water-related aspects of GlobalG.A.P. certification.
Rainforest Alliance
A certification program focused on environmental, social, and economic sustainability, particularly prevalent in tropical and subtropical agriculture (e.g., coffee, cocoa, tea, bananas). It emphasizes biodiversity conservation, improved livelihoods for farmers, and worker rights, often with a strong focus on climate-smart agriculture practices [13]. While GlobalG.A.P. covers social aspects, Rainforest Alliance has a more pronounced emphasis on social equity and environmental conservation in specific high-risk regions.
Comparison of Agricultural Certification Standards
Feature GlobalG.A.P. LEAF Marque SPRING (GlobalG.A.P. Add-on) Rainforest Alliance
Primary Focus Broad farm assurance (food safety, environment, social) Integrated Farm Management (environmental protection) Responsible water management Environmental, social, economic sustainability (tropical crops)
Scope Global, diverse agricultural sectors Primarily UK, expanding globally Global, as an add-on to GlobalG.A.P. Global, tropical/subtropical agriculture
Key Differentiator Comprehensive G.A.P. framework Whole-farm environmental approach Specialized water stewardship Biodiversity, social equity, climate-smart practices
Integration with DPPs Direct via GGN and API Potential via data exchange Via GlobalG.A.P. DPP integration Potential via data exchange

9. The EU Farm to Fork Strategy and GlobalG.A.P. Alignment

The EU Farm to Fork (F2F) Strategy, a cornerstone of the European Green Deal, aims to accelerate the transition to a sustainable food system that has a neutral or positive environmental impact, helps to mitigate climate change, and ensures food security [14]. This ambitious strategy sets targets for reducing pesticide use, increasing organic farming, improving animal welfare, and combating food waste. For agricultural producers globally, especially those exporting to the EU, alignment with F2F objectives is becoming increasingly critical.

GlobalG.A.P. standards, particularly the Integrated Farm Assurance (IFA) v6, demonstrate significant alignment with the principles and goals of the F2F Strategy. IFA v6's comprehensive coverage of topics such as integrated pest management, responsible water management, biodiversity and habitats, and efficient resource use directly contributes to the F2F's environmental objectives [4]. The continuous improvement plan embedded in IFA v6 encourages producers to adopt more sustainable practices and set measurable targets, mirroring the F2F's emphasis on progressive environmental performance.

By adhering to GlobalG.A.P. standards, South African producers can demonstrate their commitment to sustainable agriculture, thereby facilitating market access to the EU and strengthening their position in a global market increasingly driven by sustainability criteria. This alignment is not merely about compliance but also about fostering a more resilient and environmentally responsible agricultural sector.

10. How a Woolworths or Tesco Buyer Verifies a South African Producer's GlobalG.A.P. Status via DPP QR Scan

In the context of modern retail and increasing demands for supply chain transparency, a buyer from a major retailer like Woolworths (South Africa) or Tesco (UK) can leverage Digital Product Passports (DPPs) and GS1 Digital Link to verify a South African producer's GlobalG.A.P. status with unprecedented efficiency and reliability.

The process typically begins with the buyer scanning a QR code or similar 2D barcode on the product packaging. This QR code is encoded using the GS1 Digital Link standard, which, upon scanning, resolves to a unique web address (URI) [6]. This URI acts as a gateway to the product's Digital Product Passport, a comprehensive digital record containing various data points about the product's lifecycle.

Within the DPP, the buyer would find verifiable information regarding the product's GlobalG.A.P. certification. This information would include the producer's GGN and potentially a cryptographic hash of their GlobalG.A.P. certificate [7]. The DPP system, integrated with the GLOBALG.A.P. IT platform via its Public Bookmarking API, can then perform a real-time verification. By querying the API with the producer's GGN, the system can confirm the validity and scope of the GlobalG.A.P. certificate, ensuring that the producer is indeed certified for the specific product and practices claimed [5].

This seamless digital verification process eliminates the need for manual document checks, reduces the risk of fraudulent certificates, and provides instant assurance to the buyer about the product's compliance with GlobalG.A.P. standards. It enhances trust, streamlines procurement, and supports retailers in meeting their own sustainability and ethical sourcing commitments.

South African Context: Regulatory Framework and Industry Engagement

South Africa's agricultural sector operates within a dynamic regulatory and industry landscape that significantly influences the adoption and implementation of international standards like GlobalG.A.P. The country's commitment to food safety, quality, and sustainable trade is reflected in various legislative instruments and the proactive engagement of key industry players.

Regulatory Landscape

The **Protection of Personal Information Act (POPIA) 2013** is paramount for data privacy, ensuring that personal information collected and processed within DPPs and related systems adheres to strict privacy principles [15]. Similarly, the **Electronic Communications and Transactions Act (ECTA) 2002** provides the legal framework for electronic transactions and data interchange, underpinning the legality and enforceability of digital records within the DPP ecosystem [16].

Other relevant governmental bodies and regulations include:

  • **CIPC (Companies and Intellectual Property Commission):** Responsible for company registration and intellectual property, crucial for formalizing agricultural businesses.
  • **SARS (South African Revenue Service):** Ensures tax compliance for agricultural enterprises.
  • **ITAC (International Trade Administration Commission):** Oversees import and export regulations, impacting market access for certified products.
  • **PPECB (Perishable Products Export Control Board):** Plays a vital role in quality assurance, inspection, and cold chain management for perishable agricultural exports, ensuring products meet international standards upon shipment [17].
  • **DAFF (Department of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries):** Now primarily the Department of Agriculture, Land Reform and Rural Development (DALRRD), responsible for agricultural policy, food security, and sustainable resource management.

Industry Engagement

South African agricultural industry bodies are instrumental in driving GlobalG.A.P. adoption. The **Citrus Growers Association (CGA)**, **Hortgro**, and the **Fresh Produce Exporters' Forum (FPEF)** actively promote GlobalG.A.P. certification among their members, recognizing its importance for international market access and competitiveness [8, 9]. The **Sustainability Initiative of South Africa (SIZA)** further complements these efforts by providing a platform for ethical and sustainable trade practices, often aligning with GlobalG.A.P. principles [10].

Organizations like **Wines of South Africa (WOSA)**, while specific to the wine industry, demonstrate the broader trend of South African agricultural sectors embracing international quality and sustainability standards. The **Johannesburg Stock Exchange (JSE)** also plays a role for publicly listed agricultural companies, where adherence to ESG (Environmental, Social, and Governance) criteria, often supported by certifications like GlobalG.A.P., is increasingly important for investor confidence.

The **Broad-Based Black Economic Empowerment (B-BBEE)** framework is also a significant consideration, influencing business practices and supply chain development within the agricultural sector, encouraging inclusive growth alongside international compliance.
